title: The Grief That Comes With Awakening: Mourning Your Old Self
meta_description: Hey. Yeah, you. I know it's late. Or maybe it's early. Doesn't matter. You're probably staring at the ceiling, heart doing that weird fluttery thing, mind
body:
The Grief That Comes With Awakening: Mourning Your Old Self

Hey. Yeah, you. I know it's late. Or maybe it's early. Doesn't matter. You're probably staring at the ceiling, heart doing that weird fluttery thing, mind racing a million miles an hour. You're wondering what the hell is happening to you, right? You feel like you're losing your mind, or maybe you already have. You're not alone. I've been there. I'm telling you, I’ve been right where you are, staring at the same ceiling, feeling that same cold dread creep up your spine.

What you're feeling, that raw, visceral ache - it's grief. Pure, unadulterated grief. But it's not for a person you lost, or a pet. It's for you. The old you. The person you were before all this started. And let me tell you, that kind of grief can feel even more isolating because no one talks about it. No one prepares you for it. They talk about awakening like it’s all sunshine and rainbows, right? Like you suddenly float on a cloud of enlightenment. Bullshit. It's messy. It's terrifying. And it hurts like hell.

You're mourning the death of your old identity. The comfortable, predictable self you knew. The one who had a clear path, or at least thought they did. Now, everything's shifted. The ground beneath you feels unstable. The old rules don't apply. The old dreams feel hollow. And you're left with this gaping void, this deep sense of loss, and a terrifying freedom you never asked for. It's like you woke up in a different body, in a different life, and everyone else is still living in the old one. Know what I mean?

I remember those nights. The panic attacks that would hit out of nowhere. The feeling that my brain was being rewired, sometimes painfully. The intense loneliness even when I was surrounded by people. Because how do you explain this? How do you tell someone, "Hey, I think I'm dying, but not really, and also I'm becoming something else, and it's tearing me apart"? You can't. Not to most people. They'll look at you like you've gone mad. So you suffer in silence. But you don't have to. I'm here, telling you, you're not mad. You're going through something real. Something incredibly difficult, but real.

The Shattering of Identity

Here's the thing: when you go through an awakening, especially something as intense as a kundalini crisis, your old self doesn't just evolve. It gets annihilated. It's not a gentle shedding; it's an explosion. Every belief you held, every assumption about who you were, what your purpose was, how the world worked - it all gets blown to smithereens. And that's terrifying. Imagine your entire house, your safe space, suddenly crumbling around you. That's what it feels like internally.

You might find yourself questioning everything. Your career, your relationships, your hobbies, even your taste in music. Things that once brought you joy now feel meaningless. Conversations that used to engage you now feel shallow and pointless. You might feel a deep disconnect from friends and family who don't understand what you're going through. And that's okay. It’s not a sign that you're broken; it's a sign that you're changing, drastically. You're seeing the world with new eyes, and those old glasses just don't fit anymore.

I remember looking at my old photo albums, seeing the person I was, and feeling like I was looking at a stranger. A ghost. There was a detachment, a sense of "that was me, but it's not me now." It's a strange, disorienting feeling. You grieve that person because they were familiar. They were safe. They knew how to deal with the world you used to inhabit. And now, you're a stranger in a strange land, even if it's the same physical place. That’s why it’s so important to understand that what you're experiencing is a form of grief. It gives it a name, a shape, something you can begin to process.

The Discomfort of the In-Between

This phase you're in - it's the in-between. You're not the old you, but you're not fully the new you either. You're in a liminal space, a void. And humans hate voids. We crave certainty, structure, definition. This lack of definition is what makes it so incredibly uncomfortable. You feel like you're floating, untethered. You might experience periods of intense anxiety, depression, or even depersonalization. Your sense of self can feel fragmented, like a jigsaw puzzle with half the pieces missing and no picture on the box.

It's like being born again, but you're fully conscious of the birthing process, and it's agonizing. You're shedding layers, skin, old ways of being, and it's painful. You might find yourself withdrawing from social situations because you just don't have the energy to pretend everything's fine. You might feel a deep urge to be alone, to process, to just be with the intensity of what's happening. Don't fight that urge. Give yourself permission to retreat. This isn't selfishness; it's self-preservation. You're undergoing a massive internal renovation, and you need space for that.

I spent months just walking, sitting by myself, journaling. Journaling became my lifeline. Seriously, if you're not doing it, start. Just writing down whatever comes to mind, no filter, no judgment. It helps to externalize some of the chaos. You can use something basic, or if you want to get serious about it, something like Leuchtturm1917 Hardcover Dotted Notebook - A5 (paid link) can feel really good. Or, if you're looking for a more structured approach, The Artist's Way Morning Pages Journal by Julia Cameron (paid link) is a classic for a reason. Just get it out of your head and onto paper. It helps you see patterns, process emotions, and track your progress, even if it feels like you're not making any.

Processing the Loss

So, what do you do with this grief? You feel it. You allow it. You don't push it away, you don't intellectualize it. You let it wash over you, even if it feels overwhelming. Because the only way out is through. You can't bypass grief. It demands to be felt. And when you're going through something like this, when your entire sense of reality is shifting, that grief can come in waves. One minute you're okay, the next you're sobbing uncontrollably for no apparent reason. That's normal. That's the old self crying out as it dissolves.

Here are some things that helped me, and might help you:

  • Acknowledge it: Just saying, "I'm grieving my old self" out loud, even to an empty room, can be incredibly validating. It gives voice to the unspoken pain.
  • Talk to someone who gets it: This is hard, I know. But if you can find one person - a therapist who understands spiritual emergence, a spiritual director, or another survivor - cling to them. Having someone witness your experience without judgment is invaluable. If you can't find someone in person, there are online communities. Just be careful and discerning. Not everyone understands the depth of this stuff. You might even find help in books like Waking Up Is Hard to Do: Navigating Spiritual Emergence (paid link) which can make you feel less alone.
  • Allow yourself to feel everything: Anger, sadness, confusion, fear, despair. Don't judge these emotions. They're part of the process. Trying to suppress them only prolongs the suffering.
  • Practice self-compassion: You wouldn't tell a friend who lost a loved one to "just get over it," would you? Don't do it to yourself. Be kind. Be patient. You're going through a deep transformation.
  • Find healthy outlets: Exercise, nature, creative expression. These things aren't just distractions; they're ways to process energy and emotion. I found coloring to be surprisingly grounding. Something like a Mandala Coloring Book for Adults - Stress Relief (paid link) can be really helpful. It’s simple, repetitive, and calms the nervous system.
  • Rest: Your system is working overtime. Sleep. Nap. Don't push yourself. Your body and mind need time to integrate these massive shifts.

Truth is, this isn't a linear process. You'll have good days and bad days. Days where you feel like you're making progress, and days where you feel like you've fallen back into the abyss. That's okay. It's part of the journey. Don't beat yourself up for it. Just keep showing up for yourself, one moment at a time.

Integration, Not Eradication

You're not trying to get rid of the old you completely. That's impossible. Parts of that person, the good parts, the lessons learned, the experiences gained - they're all part of the foundation for the new you. The goal isn't eradication; it's integration. It's about bringing the shattered pieces back together in a new, more expansive way. It's about taking the essence of who you were and weaving it into the fabric of who you're becoming.

This is where the real work begins. Once the initial shock and grief start to subside, you begin to rebuild. But you're not building the same house. You're building a mansion, or maybe a treehouse, or something entirely new that you couldn't have even conceived of before. You're building it with new materials, new blueprints, and a deeper understanding of yourself and the universe.

It means questioning your old motivations. Why did you do what you did? What drove you? What were you seeking? Often, you'll find that many of your old pursuits were rooted in ego, in seeking external validation, in trying to fill an internal void. Now, that void feels different. It's not a lack; it's an opening. It's a space for something new to emerge. And that's both exhilarating and terrifying, right?

Finding Your New Ground

So how do you find your new ground? Slowly. Deliberately. You start by listening to that quiet inner voice that's trying to guide you. It's probably been there all along, but you were too busy, too distracted, too caught up in the noise of the old self to hear it. Now, with the old structures crumbling, that voice gets a little louder. Pay attention to what genuinely feels right, what brings you a sense of peace or expansion, even if it doesn't make logical sense to your old way of thinking.

It might mean changing careers. It might mean ending relationships that no longer serve your highest good. It might mean moving to a new place. It might mean embracing entirely new hobbies or interests. These changes can be scary, especially when you're already feeling vulnerable. But here's the thing: you can't put new wine into old wineskins. You can't build a new life on the foundations of the old one if those foundations are no longer stable or authentic to who you are now.

I'll tell you what, it's not easy. There will be moments of doubt, moments where you wish you could go back to the blissful ignorance of your old life. And that's okay. Acknowledge those feelings, but don't dwell in them. Remember why you're here, why this is happening. You're not being punished; you're being upgraded. You're being called to something greater, something more authentic, something more aligned with your true nature.

Embracing the Unknown

The hardest part, honestly, is learning to live with uncertainty. The old you probably had a plan, a timeline, expectations. The new you has to learn to surrender to the flow, to trust that there's a larger intelligence at play, even when you can't see the next step. It's like walking in the dark, with only a tiny flashlight, and you can only see a few feet in front of you. That's the spiritual journey. You just keep taking the next right step, even if you don't know where it's leading.

This isn't about finding all the answers. It's about learning to live with the questions. It's about becoming comfortable with not knowing. It's about developing a deep, unwavering trust in yourself and in something greater than yourself. Whatever you call that - God, Universe, Source, Consciousness - it's there, guiding you. You just have to learn to listen, to feel, to intuit.

The grief will lessen over time. It won't disappear entirely, just like you still remember a loved one you've lost. But it will transform. It will become a quiet ache, a bittersweet memory of who you once were. And in its place, you'll find a deep sense of peace, a quiet strength, and a deep knowing that you're exactly where you're supposed to be. You'll realize that the death of your old self wasn't an ending; it was a necessary clearing, a sacred space created for the birth of something far more beautiful, far more real. You're not losing yourself; you're finding yourself, for the very first time. And that, my friend, is a journey worth taking, no matter how hard it feels right now.

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
